title: Emergency: Cat Vomiting and Unable to Urinate | Vet Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is vomiting and unable to urinate, this is a life-threatening medical emergency. Seek immediate veterinary care to prevent fatal outcomes.
content_html:
What is Urethral Obstruction?

When a cat is vomiting and unable to pass urine, it is often a sign of a life-threatening condition called a urethral obstruction, or being "blocked." This occurs when the urethra is physically obstructed by crystals, stones, or inflammatory debris, preventing the bladder from emptying. This leads to a dangerous buildup of toxins and potassium in the bloodstream.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This is a medical emergency of the highest priority. An inability to urinate is fatal if not treated quickly. The addition of vomiting suggests that toxins are already circulating in the system, which can lead to kidney failure or cardiac arrest within hours. Do not wait for an appointment; take your cat to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a quick photo of your cat's posture or the contents of the litter box can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary triage team. A photo showing a cat in a hunched, straining position or a litter box with only small drops of blood-tinged urine helps professionals confirm the urgency of the situation and prepares the medical team to stabilize your pet the moment you arrive.

faqs.question: How long can a cat survive without urinating?
faqs.answer: A cat can suffer permanent organ damage or death within 24 to 48 hours of being unable to urinate. This condition is considered a critical emergency.

faqs.question: Is a urinary blockage more common in male or female cats?
faqs.answer: While it can happen to any cat, urethral obstructions are significantly more common in male cats because their urethras are much narrower and longer than those of females.

faqs.question: What are other signs of a urinary blockage?
faqs.answer: In addition to vomiting and straining, you may notice your cat crying out in pain, hiding, or excessively licking their genital area.
